Why risky: Normal tests can be overgeneralized beyond timing/scope.
Guardrail: Show test scope/date and symptom status; require unresolved-symptom safety net.

Why risky: More alerts can hide abnormal results and worsen clinician burden.
Guardrail: Risk ranking, deduplication, owner/deadline, false-positive review.

Why risky: Users may treat suggestions as final diagnoses.
Guardrail: Support-only language, source links, uncertainty, clinician review and override.

Why risky: Models may reproduce dismissal by age, gender, race, mental-health or difficult-patient labels.
Guardrail: Anti-bias audits; no stigmatizing label rules; physical red-flag review when attribution is discordant.

Why risky: Family history, finances, immigration, mental health and patient photos are sensitive.
Guardrail: Consent, minimization, provenance, role-based access, patient correction/deletion where permitted.

Why risky: Persistent symptom flags may cause anxiety or unnecessary testing.
Guardrail: Plain uncertainty language; clinician-reviewed next steps; measure utilization and harm.

Why risky: Capturing stockouts/referral failures could blame clinics/CHWs for infrastructure gaps.
Guardrail: Non-punitive resource reporting and manager dashboards; alternate plans and referral mapping.

Why risky: Dismissal flags can feel accusatory and escalate conflict.
Guardrail: Use “unresolved concern” language; preserve patient voice separately; mediation/correction path.

Why risky: A concise summary may omit rare but critical details.
Guardrail: Source-linked expandable details, uncertainty field, clinician confirmation, audit misses.

Why risky: A tracker without owner/deadline is another passive record.
Guardrail: Owner, backup owner, due date, escalation and closure reason required for safety-critical tasks.
